food_name: Chè Đậu Xanh Đánh (Vietnamese Mashed Mung Bean Sweet Soup)
aliases: Vietnamese mashed mung bean sweet soup
Mung bean sweet pudding with coconut milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chè đậu xanh đánh is approximately 250 g, typically served chilled or at room temperature in a small bowl or cup.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mung beans, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt (FDC ID 175238) and Coconut milk, raw (FDC ID 170172); values adapted by weighted ingredient composition for chè đậu xanh đánh
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chè đậu xanh đánh exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Values are adapted from USDA FDC entries for cooked mung beans (FDC ID 175238) and coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), combined with standard sugar addition, weighted to reflect a typical preparation of approximately 55% mung bean paste, 25% coconut milk, and 20% sugar syrup. Input record values (energy 140 kcal, protein 3.4 g, fat 6.0 g, carbohydrate 18.0 g, sugar 12.0 g, sodium 32 mg, dietary fibre 2.0 g, saturated fat 4.8 g) are consistent with this composition model and are retained. Calcium 12 mg, iron 0.6 mg, cholesterol 0 mg, trans fat 0 g, and vitamin C 0 mg are also consistent with USDA source data for this ingredient combination.

gi_source.title: Mung bean (Vigna radiata), cooked — GI value 39 (glucose reference); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chè đậu xanh đánh as a composite dish. GI of 39 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked mung beans (Vigna radiata), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. The addition of sugar (sucrose, GI ~65) and coconut milk (negligible carbohydrate contribution) would moderately elevate the effective GI of the dish above the pure mung bean value; however, the mung bean starch matrix and fibre content (2 g/100 g) are expected to attenuate this effect. GI 39 is therefore used as a conservative lower-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (39 × 45.0) / 100 = 17.6 (carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 18.0 × 2.5 = 45.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (12 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage/dessert context independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 32 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high added sugar content (12 g/100 g), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines on sugar-sweetened beverages; an unsweetened mung bean preparation with reduced coconut milk would be a suitable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar at 12 g/100 g is a hard disqualifier for Type 2 Diabetes management; despite mung beans having a low GI (39), the sugar load drives postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish in its standard sweetened form, as the 12 g/100 g added sugar content (30 g per 250 g serving) will cause significant postprandial glucose elevation; an unsweetened preparation using only mung beans and pandan without added sugar or coconut milk would be a low-GI, diabetes-compatible alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.8 g/100 g from coconut milk and added sugar at 12 g/100 g both contribute to cardiovascular risk; cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally due to its saturated fat content (4.8 g/100 g from coconut milk) and high added sugar (12 g/100 g), both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and increased CVD risk; requesting preparation with reduced coconut milk and minimal added sugar would substantially lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chè đậu xanh đánh (chè đậu xanh đánh) is a traditional Vietnamese sweet dessert soup or pudding made from split, hulled mung beans (Vigna radiata) that are boiled until fully soft and then vigorously mashed or whipped ('đánh' means 'to beat/whip') to create a smooth, creamy paste. The dish is sweetened with white sugar and enriched with coconut milk or coconut cream, giving it a rich, velvety texture and a mildly sweet, nutty flavour with a subtle coconut aroma. It is commonly served chilled with crushed ice, making it a popular street food and dessert during warm weather. Pandan leaves are often added during cooking to impart a fragrant, grassy note. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 140 kcal, 3.4 g protein, 6.0 g fat (4.8 g saturated from coconut milk), 18.0 g carbohydrate, 12.0 g sugar, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 32 mg sodium. Clinically, the high added sugar content (12 g/100 g) makes this dish unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension in its standard form. The saturated fat from coconut milk (4.8 g/100 g) is a moderate cardiovascular concern. Mung beans themselves are a nutritious legume with a low glycemic index (GI ~39), and an unsweetened preparation would be suitable for most dietary conditions. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for chè đậu xanh đánh exists in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked mung beans and coconut milk, weighted by typical ingredient proportions. The input record values are consistent with this composition model and have been retained. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked mung beans as the dominant carbohydrate source. Medium confidence reflects the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese dessert preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as a dish-level entry. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this specific composite dish preparation in any approved source consulted (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, FAO/INFOODS SEA). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this plant-based preparation with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA FDC data for mung beans and coconut milk. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; consistent with USDA FDC data. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible — mung beans and coconut milk contain trace or no vitamin C after cooking; consistent with USDA FDC data. 6. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Beverages' to 'Desserts' as chè đậu xanh đánh is a semi-solid pudding/dessert soup, not a beverage. 7. GI of 39 is a lower-bound analogous estimate based on cooked mung beans; the effective dish GI may be moderately higher due to added sucrose. 8. Input provenance was 'grabfood' / 'ai_generated'; all nutrient values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central and are consistent with the approved source model.
